sentences.text: Good morning, sir.
William Dowdell Denson.
D-E-N-S-O-N.
Same.
Other than Bill?
That makes me 82, going on 83.
This is Lawrence, Nassau County, New York state.
Well, I was born and reared in Birmingham, Alabama.
I attended the public schools in Birmingham and graduated in January 1930 from John Herbert Phillips High School in Birmingham.
In July, I entered the United States Military Academy at West Point, New York, and became a cadet there, and I graduated from the Military Academy in June of 1934.
I resigned my commission as a second lieutenant in the United States Army in July of 1934 and entered Harvard Law School in September of that year.
I graduated from Harvard in 1937, in June.
I went back to Birmingham, started studying for the bar exam in Alabama.
And I took that bar exam and passed it, and was admitted to practice law in October 1937.
I, of course, entered into my father's law office, my father being a lawyer.
As a matter of fact, my grandfather on both sides of the house-- my maternal and my paternal grandparents were lawyers.

Indeed, my grandfather on my mother's side was Chief Justice of the Supreme Court of Alabama, and my grandfather on my father's side was the United States District Attorney for the Northern District of Alabama and was a member of Congress.

And speaking of Congress, my great-grandfather on my mother's side was a congressman representing the Middle District of Alabama and was a congressman from 1851 or '52 up until Alabama seceded from the Union at the time of the Civil War.
And that grand-- great-grandparent later became the president of what is now known as Auburn.
And both sides of the house have been lawyers or judges and positions of that type.
As a child, I-- there was nothing abnormally good or bad.
I grew up like most weeds do in the South.
I had a perfectly normal childhood.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I have an older sister Nell and a younger brother, Render.
My brother is dead, having passed away in 1992, and my sister is an invalid in a nursing home in Massachusetts.
She is three years older than I am.
We were a closely-knit family.
My brother also graduated from West Point in 1937.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had a good time with him when he was a plebe at West Point and I was a first classman, what you would normally know as a senior.
And those days were precious to me.
Of course.
Our next door neighbor were the Lowens, and they were a very fine Jewish family.
There was a family by the name of Josephs that lived across the street from where we were.
I had some very dear friends who were Jewish.
Ian Friend, Jerome Coppel, just to name two-- come to mind at this particular point.
But we all played together, visited in each other's homes, and enjoyed the company of each other.
I did not know the names of the holidays.
I do know that there were certain days that they could not come out and play, and that, of course, interfered with our normal routine.
But that occurred not too frequently, but frequently enough to be an interruption.
No, sir, I never saw any evidence of that at all.
As a matter of fact, they would visit in our home, and I would visit in their homes.
I think there was more antipathy toward the Catholics than there was toward the Jews in Birmingham at that time.
Yes, indeed.
Not only that, but we were not given spending money.
We had to memorize verses from the Bible in order to get our spending money.
We had no such thing as an allowance, and we performed chores as members of the family.
But any money that we acquired, we acquired by, when we were young, $0.10 a verse, later, quarter a verse, and on up to $1 a verse.
As a matter of fact, when I was at West Point, my father said, son, if you memorize and can recite verbatim the 11th chapter of Hebrews, I'll give you $100 in gold.
That was in December of 1931.
And I learned it.
There were 52 verses, and I learned those verses and spoke 'em and recited them to my father, and he gave me 10 $10 gold pieces.
And I took that money and gave it back to him [LAUGHS] because I thought he needed it more than I did at that time.
Presbyterian.
Well, I was president of the student body of John Herbert Phillips High School, where we had approximately 3,000 kids.
I was proud of that.
I don't know whether you would say I was qualified as a politician or not, but that was a fact.
And my brother and I had an automobile, and we rode to school, and we took a load of kids to school every day.
And I was slight of build, so there was no way in the world I can play football.
I think I weighed about 90 to 100 pounds.
We used to have little high school fraternities and had athletic competition there that I participated in, but I never was a star athlete, anything like that.
I was president of the Yancy Literary Society in high school and learned Robert's Rules of Order at that time.
So other than a normal kid growin' up, there wasn't anything that particularly slated me to be a cadet or join the military.
Well, actually, that was decided for me.
A man by the name of George Huddleston was a congressman from our district, and he was a dear friend of my father's.
And sometime in January of 1930, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mr.
Huddleston had dinner with us at the house.
And I graduated from high school in the middle of the year.
And I was courtin' a gal at that time, and I had some circles under my eyes.
And when I left that dinner table, my father says, George-- speaking to the congressman-- I don't know what I'm gonna do with that boy.
He doesn't know how to take care of himself.
And George says, send him to West Point.
And I threw up my hands, agreeing.
Said yeah, send me to West Point.
And the next thing I knew, I'd received a notice to appear at the post office for preliminary physical examination, and I attended that and passed it.
I had two ears and two eyes and all of my teeth, so that's how I got appointed to West Point.
However, there was an inducement then.

My father said, son, if you-- I was required to take what was known as not the general entrance exam, but a qualifying examination in algebra, and plane geometry, English grammar, and English and American literature.
And Dad said, if you want any coaching, I'll be glad to pay for that.
And he said, if you pass that entrance exam, I'll give you $500.
So I passed that exam, and I got my 500 bucks and went to New York, and I spent it all before I got to West Point.
I had just enough there to pay my railroad fare from Weehawken up to West Point when I entered in July of 1930.
Now, during your years at West Point, did you anticipate that you were going to be a career military officer, or had you in mind that you might go to law school someday?
No, I didn't catch on to becoming a lawyer until my senior year at West Point, where we were required to take a course of law.

It was general in nature and was directed primarily toward equipping young officers serving on courts martial as trial judge advocates and defense counsel, and, yeah, that's when I first got a feeling for it.

And during that time, they had to retire as many old officers as there were graduates in my class to make room for 'em, so they invited anybody that wanted to to actually resign and not have to serve as an officer.
And I did that after I found out that they would accept me at Harvard.
So I went from Harvard-- from West Point to Harvard in September of 1934.
I graduated during the summer.
Yes.
Yes, certainly.
Birkowitz, ?] [?
Gilman, ?] two that come to mind in my class.
No.
Absolutely none.
You were your own man in that sense, and we had to go to chapel, and there was a Jewish chapel, and a Catholic chapel, and a Protestant chapel.
They were Protestant.
Well, that was quite a transition because at West Point, their method of instruction was [? really ?] different than it was in law school.
At West Point, the cadets did the reciting of the lessons that had been assigned.
At Harvard, the professors did the lecturing in law school, and you took notes.
And I had one heck of a time trying to take notes in that transition period.
But I made it.
Oh, very enjoyable.
My first year, I lived in a dormitory the first year, and the second and third years, I had an apartment with three of my classmates.
There were four of us, and we had an apartment and a cook that cooked our meals for us, just like an extension of home, except we had to go to school.
Excuse me?
Absolutely.
Absolutely.
Well--
Yes, there was a lot of suffering.

There was a lot of-- I never had to endure it because my grub was always provided for by Uncle Sam, and I was sheltered to that extent, that I was not exposed to the suffering that did prevail during those years of the Depression.
Oh, yeah, quite a number, a lot more there than there were in West Point.
Oh yeah.
Oh, yeah, sure.
There were quite a number of them.
And all of 'em were smart as whips, too, very smart.
Oh, no.
No, Uncle Sam severed the umbilical cord with me when I resigned my commission in July of 1934.
My family paid it.
My dad paid it.
I went back to Birmingham and entered my father's law office--
--and studied for the bar exam.
Well, I had the benefit of my father's experience there, who was a very able trial lawyer, and he started me out trying lawsuits on October the 4th, the day I was admitted to the bar in Alabama.
And I had his guidance, and the things that he taught me were invaluable and to the extent that I did a tremendous amount of trial work and appellate work as well.
I didn't have to wait around.

I was able to go in and-- as soon as I was admitted to the Supreme Court of Alabama, I was eligible to argue cases in the Supreme Court, which I did, the Supreme Court being the court of last resort in Alabama.
General.
My father had a general practice.
Ambulance chasing and property.
I only had three criminal trials [LAUGHS] before I went to West Point-- when I went back there [INAUDIBLE].
One was for murder.
One was burglary in the nighttime, and the third one was armed robbery.
And I was able to get an acquittal in the armed robbery case.
And in the burglary case, my man had to plead guilty.
And he did that because he had a record a mile long.
He was an escape artist, and he said, Mr.
Denson, don't worry about that.
We'll cop a plea, as he put it, and I'll be out before long.
And the other case, the murder case, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I got him off of-- on involuntary manslaughter for three years, and that was all the criminal work I'd ever done.
Well, in-- in November of 1941, I received a letter from Colonel Bukerma who was a professor of economics and government at West Point.
He was contacting former graduates who had gone and obtained degrees or other expertise that could be used at West Point, asking if I was willing to come back to West Point and teach.
And I, of course, told him I was not unmindful of my obligations, and I would be happy to.
So it was due to his letter of November of 1941 that I went back to West Point, and they took me in in the rank of a captain, which was the rank that my class held at that time.
I thought that was mighty fine of 'em.
And I taught law there until I left to go overseas in February of 1945.
Now, when you were at West Point, was this is an equivalent rank, or were you wearing a uniform?
I was wearing a uniform.
I was back into Uncle Sam's arm.
Well, the difference basically was in the number of cadets.
That was-- there was only 1,200 cadets in the corps of cadets when I graduated, and there were 4,000 when I went back to teach there.
And they were spread over the four years.
Now, how many that would be for each class is a matter of arithmetic.
But it was an expedited course.
They had graduation in the winter, and they had a graduation in June.
So there were two graduation classes in each year that I was there.
No.
No.
No.
No.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] To tell you frankly, I didn't know what a war crime was at that time.
I knew-- we just had a smattering of Constitutional law, a little smattering of the conventions-- the Hague and Geneva Conventions.
And they were important [INAUDIBLE] because they do relate very much directly toward conduct of individuals, combatants and non-combatants.
No, sir.
No, sir.
Yes.
My personal knowledge was absolutely nothing.
I didn't have any idea of the privations that the Jewish people were being subjected to.
No discussion of that at all at that time, and that was from '41 until the time when I left there in '45.
